metaDescription: Hospitality accountants for restaurants, cafes, bars and hotels. Tips and tronc, VAT, gross profit and casual payroll handled. Fixed fees, switch in days.
title: Hospitality Accountants for Restaurants, Bars & Hotels
intro: Hospitality runs on thin margins and long hours, so the numbers have to be right and they have to be timely. We help restaurants, cafes, bars and hotels stay on top of tips, VAT, gross profit and a constantly changing payroll — with a dedicated named accountant, a fixed monthly fee and cloud-first books that link to your till so you always know where you stand.
highlights: Tips handled correctly under the new tipping law
Tronc set up to administer tips National-Insurance-efficiently
VAT on hot, cold, eat-in and takeaway sorted properly
Weekly management accounts with gross profit %
Stock control and labour-cost % kept in view
RTI payroll for seasonal and casual staff
Fixed monthly fee with your own accountant

sections.heading: The new tipping rules, handled properly
body: Tips are now tightly regulated. The Employment (Allocation of Tips) Act 2023 came into force on 1 October 2024, and it changes what employers must do with the money customers leave for staff.
We make sure your business meets the rules and keeps the records to prove it, so a popular part of the job does not become a compliance problem.
bullets: You must pass on 100% of tips to workers, with no deductions
Tips must be allocated fairly between staff
You must keep a written tips policy and the supporting records
A tronc run by an independent troncmaster can administer tips National-Insurance-efficiently

sections.heading: VAT on food and drink, applied correctly
body: Few sectors have VAT as fiddly as hospitality, where the treatment can change from one item on the menu to the next. Getting it wrong on every transaction quietly adds up to a significant error.

Most hot food and drink and anything eaten in is standard-rated at 20%, while the position on cold and takeaway items can differ. We make sure each line is treated the right way and that your returns stand up to scrutiny.

sections.heading: Tight margins and the numbers that protect them
body: In hospitality the difference between a good month and a bad one is often a few percentage points, so waiting for year-end accounts to see how you are doing is far too late.
We give you weekly management accounts and the operational measures that actually drive profit, so you can act on a slipping margin while there is still time to do something about it.
bullets: Weekly management accounts rather than an annual look back
Gross profit % tracked by site or by area of the menu
Stock control so wastage and shrinkage do not eat your margin
Labour-cost % kept in line as rotas and trade change

sections.heading: Payroll for seasonal and casual teams
body: Hospitality payroll rarely sits still. Hours change week to week, staff come and go with the seasons, and everything still has to be reported to HMRC on time and paid correctly.

We run your payroll under Real Time Information, handle starters and leavers as they happen, and make sure your pay rates stay at or above the National Minimum and National Living Wage, which is reviewed each April.

sections.heading: Your till, your cash and your books, joined up
body: Cash and card takings, deposits, refunds and float all need to reconcile, and in a busy venue that is easy to lose track of. We connect your EPOS system to your cloud books so sales flow through automatically and the day's takings tie back without a weekly paper chase.
Good cash controls sit alongside it, so you can spot a discrepancy quickly and keep the money side of the business honest and clear.

sections.heading: Chartered-standard, cloud-first, easy to switch
body: We run your books in Xero, QuickBooks or Sage with your till feeding in, so your numbers are always current across one site or several. Joining us is paperless and usually takes a few days, with your records collected under professional clearance.
We are ACCA-qualified, work to chartered standards and are fully insured, with our office in Manchester supporting hospitality businesses across the UK.

faqs.question: What do the new tipping rules mean for my business?
faqs.answer: Since the Employment (Allocation of Tips) Act 2023 came into force on 1 October 2024, you must pass on 100% of tips to workers, allocate them fairly and keep a written tips policy. We help you meet these duties and keep the records that show you have.

faqs.question: What is a tronc and should I use one?
faqs.answer: A tronc is an arrangement run by an independent troncmaster to share tips among staff, and it can administer them in a National-Insurance-efficient way. Whether it suits you depends on how your tips work. We set one up and run it correctly where it helps.

faqs.question: How is VAT charged on food and drink?
faqs.answer: Most hot food and drink and anything consumed on the premises is standard-rated at 20%, while the treatment of cold and takeaway items can differ. It varies item by item, so we make sure every sale is coded correctly and your VAT returns are accurate.

faqs.question: Can you handle payroll for casual and seasonal staff?
faqs.answer: Yes. We run your payroll under Real Time Information, deal with frequent starters and leavers and varying hours, and report to HMRC on time. We also keep your pay rates in line with the National Minimum and National Living Wage, which is reviewed each April.

faqs.question: How often will I see my numbers?
faqs.answer: Because margins are tight, we usually provide weekly management accounts showing gross profit %, labour-cost % and stock, rather than waiting for the year end. This is general guidance, and we will tailor the reporting to how your venue actually trades.

metaDescription: Accountants for locums, GPs, dentists and pharmacists. IR35 for NHS work, NHS pension and superannuation, mixed income and professional costs. Fixed fees.
title: Accountants for Locums, GPs & Healthcare
intro: Medical careers come with a tax position that is anything but simple — mixed NHS and private income, the NHS Pension Scheme, IR35 on agency work and a long list of professional costs. We help locums, GPs, dentists and pharmacists keep it all in order, with a dedicated named accountant, a fixed monthly fee and cloud-first books so you can get on with patients rather than paperwork.
highlights: Sole trader or limited company set up to suit you
IR35 and off-payroll status reviewed for NHS and agency work
NHS Pension Scheme and superannuation supported
Annual allowance and tapering charges checked
GMC, GDC, indemnity and training costs claimed
Mixed NHS and private income handled
Fixed monthly fee with your own accountant

sections.heading: Sole trader or limited company for locum work
body: Locums often work as a sole trader or through their own limited company, and the better route depends on how and where you take on work, your income and your plans.
We look at your situation and recommend the structure that genuinely fits, then keep it under review as your mix of work changes rather than leaving you in the wrong setup.

sections.heading: IR35 and off-payroll working in healthcare
body: IR35 and the off-payroll rules matter a great deal for locums who take work through agencies or directly for the NHS, because they decide whether an engagement is taxed like employment or genuine self-employment.

Where a medium or large NHS body is the end client, it decides your status for that engagement. We help you understand each determination, check it against how you actually work, and keep your affairs straight on both inside and outside engagements.
bullets: A medium or large NHS body as end client decides your status
Inside IR35: the engagement is taxed much like employment
Outside IR35: you can usually work through your own company in the normal way
Status turns on control, personal service and substitution, and mutuality of obligation

sections.heading: The NHS Pension Scheme and your superannuation
body: The NHS Pension Scheme is valuable, but it brings real complexity for practitioners — particularly around superannuation and the tax charges that can arise on the way contributions and growth are treated.
We support your NHS pension position, help with the superannuation figures practitioners must report, and keep an eye on the charges that catch many doctors and dentists out.
bullets: Support with NHS Pension Scheme membership and superannuation
The pension annual allowance can trigger a tax charge
Tapering of the allowance can apply for high earners
We help you understand and plan for any charge that arises

sections.heading: Claiming your professional costs in full
body: Medical professionals carry a long list of legitimate costs, and missing them means paying more tax than you need to year after year.
We make sure your allowable professional expenses are captured properly, so you claim everything you are entitled to without straying outside the rules.
bullets: GMC and GDC and professional body fees
Indemnity insurance
Training and continuing professional development
Equipment used for your work

sections.heading: Mixed income and GP partnerships
body: Many practitioners earn from more than one source — salaried NHS work alongside private practice or locum sessions — and pulling it together correctly for tax takes care.

We bring your NHS and private income into one clear picture and handle the returns that follow. Where you are part of a GP partnership, we prepare the partnership tax return and tie it back to each partner's personal position.

sections.heading: Chartered-standard, cloud-first, easy to switch
body: We run your books in Xero, QuickBooks or Sage so your numbers are always current, whether you are a single-handed locum or part of a busy practice. Joining us is paperless and usually takes a few days, with your records collected under professional clearance.
We are ACCA-qualified, work to chartered standards and are fully insured, with our office in Manchester supporting healthcare professionals across the UK.

faqs.question: Should I work as a sole trader or through a limited company?
faqs.answer: It depends on how you take on work, your income and your plans. Locums commonly use either a sole trader setup or their own limited company, each with trade-offs. We assess your circumstances and recommend the structure that genuinely suits you.

faqs.question: Who decides my IR35 status for NHS and agency work?
faqs.answer: Where a medium or large NHS body is the end client, that body decides your status for the engagement. We help you understand each determination, sense-check it against how you actually work, and keep both inside and outside engagements properly accounted for.

faqs.question: Can the NHS pension create a tax charge?
faqs.answer: It can. The pension annual allowance, and the tapering of that allowance for high earners, can give rise to tax charges linked to your NHS Pension Scheme growth. We help with the superannuation figures and plan for any charge so it does not come as a surprise.

faqs.question: What professional costs can I claim?
faqs.answer: Broadly, the costs of doing your job — GMC or GDC and professional body fees, indemnity insurance, training and equipment used for work. We review your spending so you claim everything you are entitled to while staying within HMRC's rules.

faqs.question: Do you deal with GP partnerships?
faqs.answer: Yes. We prepare the partnership tax return for the practice and tie it back to each partner's personal Self Assessment, bringing any mixed NHS and private income together. This is general guidance, not personal advice, and we tailor it to your practice.

metaDescription: Construction accountants for CIS contractors and subcontractors. Monthly CIS returns, the VAT reverse charge and gross payment status handled. Fixed fees.
title: Construction Accountants for CIS & Contractors
intro: The Construction Industry Scheme, the VAT reverse charge and the question of who counts as employed all make construction one of the more demanding sectors for tax. We help contractors and subcontractors stay compliant and keep their cash flowing — with a dedicated named accountant, a fixed monthly fee and cloud-first books that handle CIS as part of the routine, not a monthly scramble.
highlights: CIS deductions calculated and applied correctly
Monthly CIS returns filed by the 19th
Subcontractor verification and payment statements handled
Gross payment status applications and reviews
VAT domestic reverse charge handled for CIS work
CIS suffered offset against PAYE for limited companies
Fixed monthly fee with your own accountant

sections.heading: The Construction Industry Scheme, handled end to end
body: Under CIS, contractors deduct tax from what they pay subcontractors and pass it to HMRC, and the rate depends on the subcontractor's status. Getting the deduction wrong affects both sides, so it pays to have it run properly.
We calculate the deductions, apply the right rate to each subcontractor and keep the whole scheme on track for you.
bullets: 20% deducted for registered subcontractors
30% where the subcontractor is not verified or registered
0% where the subcontractor holds gross payment status
Limited-company subcontractors can offset CIS deductions suffered against their PAYE liabilities

sections.heading: Monthly CIS returns and contractor duties
body: Contractors carry several CIS obligations every month, and the deadlines are fixed. Missing a return brings automatic penalties, so we build the work around the date and keep you ahead of it.
We verify your subcontractors with HMRC before you pay them, prepare and file the monthly return, and issue the payment and deduction statements your subcontractors need.
bullets: Monthly CIS returns due by the 19th of the month
Subcontractors verified with HMRC before payment
Payment and deduction statements issued to subcontractors
Deductions reported and reconciled with HMRC

sections.heading: The VAT domestic reverse charge for construction
body: The VAT domestic reverse charge for construction services has applied since 1 March 2021, and it changed how VAT moves between businesses in the supply chain.

Rather than the supplier charging VAT, the customer accounts for it instead. We make sure your invoices and returns reflect the reverse charge correctly, so neither over- nor under-charged VAT trips you up.

sections.heading: Gross payment status, worth getting right
body: Gross payment status lets a subcontractor be paid in full without CIS deductions, which can make a real difference to cash flow. It is not automatic, though — there are tests to pass and a standard to maintain.
We help you apply where you qualify, prepare for the turnover and compliance tests, and keep your affairs in the shape needed to hold on to the status once you have it.
bullets: Gross payment status has turnover and compliance tests to meet
Being paid gross can significantly improve cash flow
Your filing and payment record must stay clean to keep it
We handle the application and ongoing reviews

sections.heading: Employment status on site
body: Whether a worker is genuinely a subcontractor, an employee or caught by IR35 is a question that comes up constantly in construction, and getting it wrong is expensive.
We help you assess employment status correctly so labour is treated the right way for tax, keeping both you and your workers on the right side of HMRC.

sections.heading: Chartered-standard, cloud-first, easy to switch
body: We run your books in Xero, QuickBooks or Sage with CIS built into the workflow, so deductions, returns and statements are handled as a matter of routine. Joining us is paperless and usually takes a few days, with your records collected under professional clearance.
We are ACCA-qualified, work to chartered standards and are fully insured, with our office in Manchester supporting construction businesses across the UK.

faqs.question: What rate of CIS deduction applies to my subcontractors?
faqs.answer: It depends on their status. The deduction is 20% for registered subcontractors, 30% where a subcontractor is not verified or registered, and 0% for those with gross payment status. We verify each subcontractor and apply the correct rate for you.

faqs.question: When are CIS returns due?
faqs.answer: Monthly CIS returns are due by the 19th of the month. As a contractor you also need to verify subcontractors before paying them and issue payment and deduction statements. We prepare and file the return and handle these duties as part of the service.

faqs.question: How does the VAT reverse charge for construction work?
faqs.answer: Since 1 March 2021, the VAT domestic reverse charge means the customer accounts for the VAT on most construction services rather than the supplier charging it. We make sure your invoices and VAT returns apply the reverse charge correctly.

faqs.question: Can I get paid without CIS deductions?
faqs.answer: Yes, if you hold gross payment status, which lets you be paid in full and settle your tax later. It has turnover and compliance tests, and you must keep a clean record to retain it. We handle the application and help you meet the conditions.

faqs.question: I run a limited company — what happens to my CIS deductions?
faqs.answer: Limited-company subcontractors can offset the CIS deductions suffered against their PAYE liabilities, rather than waiting until the year end to recover them. We manage this through your payroll so your cash position is not tied up unnecessarily.

metaDescription: Accountants for creative agencies and studios. Project profitability, R&D and creative-industry tax reliefs, WIP and IR35 handled. Fixed fees, switch in days.
title: Accountants for Creative Agencies & Studios
intro: Creative work is brilliant at making things and notoriously tricky at tracking the money behind them — lumpy cash flow, half-finished projects and a shifting mix of staff and freelancers. We help design, marketing, media and studio businesses see where they actually make money, with a dedicated named accountant, a fixed monthly fee and cloud-first books that turn busy into profitable.
highlights: Project profitability tracked job by job
Work-in-progress and time tracking kept accurate
Retainer and project billing handled cleanly
Cash flow smoothed across lumpy months
Freelancer and subcontractor mix and IR35 reviewed
R&D and creative-industry tax reliefs explored
Fixed monthly fee with your own accountant

sections.heading: Knowing which projects actually make money
body: It is easy for a creative business to be flat out and still not sure which work pays. The headline fee on a project rarely tells you the real story once the hours that went into it are counted.
We track profitability job by job using your time and cost data, so you can see your true margin on each project and price the next one with confidence.
bullets: Project profitability measured against the time and costs that went in
Work-in-progress valued so unbilled effort is not invisible
Time tracking that ties hours back to jobs and clients
A clear view of your most and least profitable work

sections.heading: Retainers, projects and lumpy cash flow
body: Creative income tends to arrive in waves — a big project lands, then a quiet patch follows — and a mix of retainer and one-off project billing makes the pattern harder to read.
We keep your billing clean across both models and give you a forward view of cash, so a slow month is something you have planned for rather than something that catches you out.

sections.heading: Freelancers, subcontractors and IR35
body: Most agencies and studios flex their capacity with freelancers and subcontractors, which is efficient but brings questions about how those people are engaged and taxed.
We help you manage the freelancer and subcontractor mix and consider IR35 where it applies, so the people side of the business is handled correctly and does not create a hidden tax exposure.

sections.heading: R&D tax relief for genuine innovation
body: Creative businesses sometimes solve real technical problems — building tools, platforms or techniques that did not exist before — and that work can qualify for R&D tax relief.
We help you identify genuine technological advances, separate them from routine creative work, and build a claim that is properly evidenced and stands up to HMRC scrutiny.

sections.heading: Creative-industry tax reliefs
body: Beyond R&D, there are specific reliefs aimed at the creative sector, and qualifying companies can benefit meaningfully where the rules are met. They are easy to overlook, so we flag them early.
We help you check whether your work qualifies and claim correctly, alongside getting the VAT and place-of-supply position right when you work with clients abroad.
bullets: Audio-Visual Expenditure Credit for film, high-end and children's TV, and animation
Video Games Expenditure Credit, available from 2024
VAT and place-of-supply rules handled for international clients
Eligibility checked so a relief is not left unclaimed

sections.heading: Chartered-standard, cloud-first, easy to switch
body: We run your books in Xero, QuickBooks or Sage with project and time data feeding in, so your numbers are always current and decision-ready. Joining us is paperless and usually takes a few days, with your records collected under professional clearance.
We are ACCA-qualified, work to chartered standards and are fully insured, with our office in Manchester supporting creative businesses across the UK.

faqs.question: How can I tell which projects are profitable?
faqs.answer: By tracking the time and costs that go into each job against what you bill for it. We use your time and project data to measure profitability job by job and value your work-in-progress, so you can see your real margins and price future work accordingly.

faqs.question: How do you help with lumpy cash flow?
faqs.answer: Creative income often comes in waves, so we keep your retainer and project billing tidy and build a forward view of cash. That lets you plan around quieter periods and tax payments rather than reacting to them when the bank balance dips.

faqs.question: Do I need to think about IR35 with freelancers?
faqs.answer: Often, yes. Where you engage freelancers and subcontractors, IR35 and employment status can be relevant to how they are taxed. We help you manage the mix and consider IR35 where it applies, so there is no hidden exposure in how you resource projects.

faqs.question: Could my agency qualify for R&D tax relief?
faqs.answer: Possibly. R&D tax relief is for genuine technological advances — for example building new tools or platforms — rather than routine creative work. We help you identify qualifying activity and build a well-evidenced claim that stands up to HMRC scrutiny.

faqs.question: What creative-industry tax reliefs are available?
faqs.answer: Qualifying companies may claim reliefs such as the Audio-Visual Expenditure Credit for film, high-end and children's TV and animation, and the Video Games Expenditure Credit from 2024. We check eligibility and claim correctly. This is general guidance, not personal advice.
